one_line_takeaway: ALERT detects unseen LLM jailbreaks with zero jailbreak examples in training by amplifying benign-vs-jailbreak signals in shallow-layer gated-FFN 'gating/context' features (not hidden states) and classifying with two VIB heads, beating the best prior detector by 10-40% while most baselines fall to near-random.
key_finding: Under the zero-shot setting many existing detectors collapse to near-random (~50% Acc / near-0 F1), confirming the setting's difficulty. ALERT consistently ranks in the top two across every dataset/attack/LLM and beats the second-best baseline by at least 10% in both Acc and F1 on average, with the largest gap on Mistral (the paper reports ~40% Acc / ~30% F1; concretely ~39% Acc over FJD which is second-best by Acc at 55.57, and ~27% F1 over self-Ex which is second-best by F1 at 67.40). Averaged over datasets/attacks it reaches 96.34 Acc / 96.49 F1 on LLaMA-3, 94.37/94.36 on Mistral, and 90.04/90.41 on Vicuna-v1.5. Ablation (Table 3, AdvBench/LLaMA-3): with all mechanisms removed, AutoDAN detection collapses to 48.56 Acc / 0.00 F1; adding layer-only barely helps (49.04/0.00), adding module-wise jumps to 93.75/93.46, and the full model reaches 97.60/97.58 — module-wise amplification (gating+context features) contributes the single largest gain.
core_contribution: The paper (ALERT) formalizes a new, harder detection setting the authors call zero-shot jailbreak detection: the detector is trained ONLY on benign and plain-harmful prompts (no jailbreak templates ever seen at training) and must flag jailbreak prompts at test time. This is contrasted with prior full-shot detection (test attack templates also appear in training) and few-shot detection (a subset of templates seen). The authors' own method, ALERT, is a model-agnostic, plug-and-play detector built on three internal-representation 'amplification' mechanisms that progressively magnify the benign-vs-jailbreak discrepancy: (1) LAYER-wise — using log-scaled symmetric KL divergence over hidden states, they find shallow layers (3rd-4th) are most safety-sensitive and fix layer 4 as the target; (2) MODULE-wise — inside the gated FFN (h = context feature LIN_c(x) elementwise-multiplied by gated feature sigma(LIN_g(x))), they show the intermediate 'gating' and 'context' features are far more zero-shot-discriminative than the commonly used hidden states, and train two independent Variational Information Bottleneck (VIB) classifiers f_g and f_c whose outputs are averaged; (3) TOKEN-wise — they down-weight semantically noisy jailbreak-template tokens via softmax weights based on L2 distance to benign/harmful prototype vectors, replacing naive token-averaging. NOTE: HiddenDetect's finding that lower layers are safety-relevant is PRIOR work the paper builds beyond, not ALERT's own discovery — ALERT extends it by showing jailbreak attacks do not disrupt shallow-layer safety activations.
threat_model: Defender operates an aligned LLM and wants a cheap inference-time filter that flags jailbreak prompts (harmful instructions wrapped in attack templates) BEFORE/while the model responds. Adversary crafts jailbreak prompts using attack families the defender has never catalogued — the realistic case where new attacks (AutoDAN, Adaptive Attack, CodeChameleon, etc.) continually emerge. The detector reads the LLM's internal activations in a single forward pass; it must (per the paper's three 'practicality principles') generalize to unseen attacks (generalizability), stay lightweight/single-pass/early-layer (efficiency), and not modify the prompt so benign response quality is untouched (innocuousness). Jailbreak and plain-harmful prompts share label y=1; benign are y=0.
limitations: Author-stated: ALERT uses a relatively simple VIB detector and does not yet apply domain-adaptation/generalization techniques to explicitly close the harmful-vs-jailbreak domain gap; the gating/context-feature insight is proposed only as a future mitigation direction, not yet exploited for defense/post-training. Reviewer-observed: the Figure-5 MOTIVATING experiment that justifies token-wise amplification relies on manually separating jailbreak template from harmful instruction, though the DEPLOYED weighting (Eq. 5-6) is automatic (softmax over L2 distance to benign/harmful prototypes) and needs no such split at test time; layer/module choices (layer 4, gated-FFN structure) are tuned on LLaMA-3 and assume a gated-activation FFN, so transfer to non-gated architectures is untested; all three attacks evaluated are optimization/encoding-style and the detector is not stress-tested against adaptive attacks that target the detector itself; Vicuna results on XSTest (68.35 Acc AutoDAN) show the margin is not always large.

quantitative_results: ALERT average across datasets/attacks: LLaMA-3-8B 96.34 Acc / 96.49 F1; Mistral-7B 94.37 Acc / 94.36 F1; Vicuna-v1.5-7B 90.04 Acc / 90.41 F1
ALERT best per-cell up to 99.04 Acc / 99.04 F1 (LLaMA-3, AdvBench, Adaptive and Chameleon)
Mistral: paper claims ALERT beats second-best by ~40% Acc / ~30% F1; second-best by Acc is FJD (55.57 -> ~39% gap to ALERT 94.37), second-best by F1 is self-Ex (67.40 -> ~27% gap to ALERT 94.36)
Under zero-shot many baselines near-random: e.g. JBShield LLaMA-3 avg 50.41 Acc / 4.34 F1; GradSafe Mistral avg 52.57 / 7.41; G-Cuff Mistral avg 25.64 / 11.28
Ablation (AdvBench, LLaMA-3, AutoDAN): no amplification 48.56 Acc / 0.00 F1 -> +layer 49.04 / 0.00 -> +module 93.75 / 93.46 -> +token (full) 97.60 / 97.58
Ablation full ALERT exceeds 97% Acc and F1 on all three attacks (AutoDAN 97.60/97.58, Adaptive 99.04/99.04, Chameleon 99.04/99.04)
Zero-shot discriminative channels: gating/context features show benign RD>1 while jailbreak RD bounded <1.0; hidden-state RD for benign and harmful both confined <1.0 (heavy mass at 0)
Layer-wise KL divergence peaks at 3rd-4th layers; layer 4 chosen as target
